personality_voice.book_baseline: Hagrid is warm, emotionally expressive, and fiercely loyal — particularly to Dumbledore and Harry. He speaks in a distinctive dialect (dropped consonants, non-standard grammar), uses exclamations like 'Gallopin' Gorgons' and 'Gulpin' gargoyles', and oscillates between booming anger and tender sentimentality. He is impulsive and easily provoked to outrage (leaping up when Vernon insults James and Lily, thundering at Dumbledore's name being mocked). He is also naively indiscreet — he lets slip Nicolas Flamel's name, reveals how to calm Fluffy, and is manipulated into giving away the Stone's secret over drinks. He shows genuine self-reproach when he realizes his mistakes. He is enthusiastic about dangerous creatures to the point of irrationality (calling Norbert 'beautiful', singing it lullabies, packing its teddy bear). His emotional range is wide: he weeps openly over Harry being left with the Dursleys, howls with grief over Lily and James, and beams with pride at Harry's fame.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the dialect, the warmth, the loyalty to Dumbledore, the emotional expressiveness (sniffling at Privet Drive, sobbing over Norbert), the impulsive anger (bending Vernon's gun, giving Dudley a pig's tail), and the creature enthusiasm. Robbie Coltrane's performance captures the booming voice, the gentle giant quality, and the indiscretion (letting slip Fluffy's name, Nicolas Flamel, and the music trick). The film retains key exclamatory speech and the pattern of blurting secrets then immediately regretting them.
personality_voice.difference: The film omits some of the more colourful dialect exclamations ('Gallopin' Gorgons', 'Gulpin' gargoyles', 'Codswallop'). The extended emotional texture of the book — Hagrid's prolonged weeping at the hospital wing, his detailed self-flagellation ('I should be chucked out an' made ter live as a Muggle!') — is compressed but not eliminated. The film does not show Hagrid's sideways looks at Harry when avoiding uncomfortable truths (e.g. about Snape), which in the book signals his awareness of things he won't say. These are minor losses of secondary texture rather than core trait changes.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The dominant traits — warmth, loyalty, impulsive anger, emotional openness, indiscretion, creature obsession, distinctive voice — are all present and observable in the film corpus. The losses are in secondary texture and some dialect colour. A minor deduction is warranted for the loss of the knowing-but-evasive quality (the sideways looks) and some dialect richness, but the core personality and voice are intact.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Hagrid serves multiple structural functions in Book 1: (1) he is the primary agent who delivers Harry to the wizarding world, physically and informationally — he retrieves Harry, explains his history, introduces Diagon Alley, Gringotts, and Hogwarts; (2) he is the inadvertent plot catalyst whose indiscretion (Fluffy's weakness, Flamel's name, the dragon egg) drives the central mystery forward; (3) he is a recurring emotional anchor for Harry at Hogwarts (tea visits, Quidditch support, Christmas gift); (4) he is the authority figure in the Forbidden Forest sequence, leading the group, managing danger, and providing exposition about unicorn blood. His agency is consistently active: he initiates visits, provides information, makes decisions, and causes plot-relevant consequences.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves all four functions. Hagrid retrieves Harry, explains his history (condensed but present), guides him through Diagon Alley, and collects the Philosopher's Stone from Gringotts. His indiscretion drives the plot (Fluffy's name, Flamel, the music trick). He leads the forest sequence. The tea-visit relationship with Harry is compressed to a single scene but present. His role as plot catalyst through naivety is fully intact.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film removes the extended Diagon Alley tour (buying books, the Apothecary, the conversation about Quidditch and houses, the ice cream scene with Malfoy). This reduces Hagrid's role as Harry's primary guide and explainer of the wizarding world. The book's scene where Hagrid defends Muggle-born wizards against Malfoy's implied snobbery ('some o' the best I ever saw were the only ones with magic in 'em') is absent. The hospital wing scene where Hagrid arrives sobbing and gives Harry the photo album is compressed in the film to a brief farewell at the station — the emotional weight of his remorse and the gift are present but reduced. The book's scene where Hagrid notices Harry is quiet on the train and reassures him ('Don' you worry, Harry. You'll learn fast enough') is replaced by a Leaky Cauldron scene that partially covers the same ground.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The core narrative functions are preserved. The reduction of the Diagon Alley guide role and the compression of the hospital wing scene represent a modest loss of agency in the 'explainer and emotional anchor' function, but no action is transferred to another character. The penalty is minor.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Hagrid's motivations are consistent and clear: fierce loyalty to Dumbledore (he obeys Dumbledore's instructions without question and defends him aggressively), love for Harry as a surrogate family member (he knew Harry's parents, delivered him as a baby, feels responsible for him), love of magical creatures (irrational and overriding his better judgment with Norbert), and a desire to be trusted and useful (he is proud when Dumbledore gives him important tasks, embarrassed by his expulsion). His internal conflict is primarily between his loyalty/discretion and his inability to keep secrets — he knows he shouldn't talk but cannot help himself. He also experiences genuine guilt and remorse when his indiscretion endangers Harry.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: All core motivations are present in the film. Loyalty to Dumbledore is explicitly stated and demonstrated. Love for Harry is shown through the birthday cake, the photo album, and emotional scenes. Creature obsession drives the Norbert subplot. Pride in being trusted is shown at Gringotts ('Professor Dumbledore gave me this'). The guilt over the Fluffy secret is present in the forest scene and implied in the Hagrid-visits-Harry scene (though compressed). The tension between wanting to be discreet and being unable to is directly evidenced in multiple scenes.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not show Hagrid's internal awareness that he is avoiding Harry's eyes when discussing Snape — a subtle signal in the book that Hagrid knows more than he says and is conflicted about it. This is a minor loss of motivational texture. The hospital wing remorse scene is compressed but the core guilt is present.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified slightly (the knowing-but-evasive quality is reduced) but remain correct and recognizable. No motivation is changed or replaced. Minor simplification warrants a small deduction from the top band.

character_arc.book_baseline: Hagrid's arc in Book 1 is relatively flat in terms of fundamental character change — he begins and ends as the same warm, loyal, indiscreet giant. However, there is a minor arc of consequence: his naivety and indiscretion (the dragon egg, Fluffy's secret) directly endanger Harry, and he experiences genuine remorse and self-awareness about this. The arc is less about transformation and more about the cost of his defining flaw (indiscretion born of trust and enthusiasm) being made visible to him. He ends the book having given Harry the photo album — an act of love and reparation.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves this arc. Hagrid's indiscretion causes the plot crisis, he experiences remorse (shown in the forest scene and the farewell), and he gives Harry the photo album as a reparative gesture. The arc's endpoint — Hagrid waving Harry off at the station, having made amends — is present.
character_arc.difference: The book's hospital wing scene, where Hagrid arrives in tears and explicitly articulates his guilt ('It's -- all -- my -- ruddy -- fault!'), is the emotional climax of his arc. In the film, this is replaced by the station farewell, which is warm but lacks the explicit remorse and self-recrimination. The arc's emotional peak is compressed. The photo album gift is present in both, but the context of guilt-driven reparation is less explicit in the film.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc exists and reaches the same endpoint in both versions. The compression of the remorse climax is a meaningful loss of the arc's emotional resolution, but the arc itself is not altered or eliminated. A modest deduction for the compressed emotional peak.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Hagrid's two key relationships in Book 1 are with Harry and with Dumbledore. With Harry: Hagrid is a surrogate parental figure — he knew Harry's parents, delivered him as a baby, is the first person to treat Harry as special and worthy of love, guides him into the wizarding world, and maintains an ongoing relationship (tea visits, Christmas gift, Quidditch support, the photo album). The dynamic is warm, protective, and slightly unequal (Hagrid as guide/protector, Harry as recipient). With Dumbledore: Hagrid's loyalty is absolute and defining — he trusts Dumbledore completely, defends him fiercely, and derives his sense of worth from being trusted by him.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Both relationships are preserved in dynamic and tone. The Harry relationship is shown through the birthday cake, the Diagon Alley trip, the Hogwarts arrival, the Quidditch match support, and the station farewell with the photo album. The Dumbledore loyalty is explicitly stated and demonstrated. The protective, guiding quality of the Harry relationship is intact.
key_relationships.difference: The film compresses the ongoing Harry relationship — the tea visit scene is reduced to a single brief exchange, the Christmas flute gift is absent (replaced by the photo album at the end), and the repeated pattern of Harry visiting Hagrid for comfort and information is reduced to fewer scenes. The emotional texture of the relationship — Hagrid as Harry's first real friend and surrogate family — is present but less developed than in the book.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics of both relationships are preserved. The compression of the Harry relationship reduces some of its warmth and texture but does not change its fundamental nature. A minor deduction for the reduced ongoing relationship pattern.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Hagrid's complexity in Book 1 comes from several sources: (1) the contradiction between his role as a trusted Dumbledore agent and his chronic inability to keep secrets; (2) his awareness that he is avoiding certain truths (the sideways looks, the subject-changing when Harry asks about Snape); (3) his irrational creature love that overrides his judgment and endangers others; (4) his self-awareness about his own limitations (he knows he shouldn't have been expelled, he knows he shouldn't do magic, he knows he talks too much); (5) the tenderness beneath the intimidating exterior (the scratchy kiss on baby Harry, the howl of grief, the photo album). The book also gives him comic texture through his obliviousness to Muggle norms (pointing at parking meters, getting stuck in ticket barriers, knitting on the train).
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core contradictions (trusted agent who can't keep secrets, creature love overriding judgment, self-awareness about his limits). The tenderness is present. Some comic texture is preserved (the birthday cake, the pig's tail). The film loses the extended Muggle-world comedy (ticket barriers, knitting on the train, pointing at parking meters), the knowing-but-evasive quality (sideways looks), and some of the self-aware commentary ('Shouldn'ta lost me temper', 'I'm not supposed ter do magic'). The hospital wing remorse scene, which is the fullest expression of his self-awareness and guilt, is compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The lost material — Muggle-world comedy, the evasive sideways looks, the extended self-reproach — reduces the texture of Hagrid's characterization without eliminating any core trait. The film Hagrid is slightly simpler: more straightforwardly warm and loyal, less visibly conflicted between discretion and indiscretion in the moment.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The complexity is mostly preserved. The losses are in secondary texture rather than core contradictions. The film does not flatten Hagrid into a single trait — he remains warm, loyal, indiscreet, creature-obsessed, and self-aware. A modest deduction for the lost texture.

lost_or_transferred_material: Extended Diagon Alley guide role: Hagrid's commentary on Quidditch, houses, Muggle-born wizards, and the Apothecary is absent
Muggle-world comedy: ticket barrier, knitting on the train, pointing at parking meters
The knowing-but-evasive quality: Hagrid's sideways looks when avoiding truths about Snape
Hospital wing remorse scene: Hagrid's explicit self-flagellation ('I should be chucked out an' made ter live as a Muggle!') is replaced by a compressed station farewell
Christmas flute gift: replaced by the photo album being given at the station rather than in the hospital wing context of guilt and reparation
Hagrid's reassurance of Harry on the train to King's Cross ('Don' you worry, Harry. You'll learn fast enough')
Hagrid's defence of Muggle-born wizards against Malfoy's implied snobbery
The repeated tea-visit pattern establishing Hagrid as Harry's ongoing emotional anchor at Hogwarts
Hagrid's self-aware admission about his wand ('I've still got the pieces... but I don't use them') and the nervous grip on his umbrella at Ollivander's
key_observations: The film adaptation of Hagrid in Philosopher's Stone is highly faithful. All core traits — warmth, loyalty to Dumbledore, emotional expressiveness, indiscretion, creature obsession, distinctive dialect, and the contradiction between trusted agent and chronic secret-leaker — are preserved and directly evidenced in the film corpus. Robbie Coltrane's performance captures the booming-yet-tender quality of the book character. The losses are primarily in secondary texture: the extended Muggle-world comedy, the knowing-but-evasive sideways looks, the full emotional weight of the hospital wing remorse scene, and the repeated tea-visit pattern that builds Hagrid as Harry's ongoing emotional anchor. None of these losses transfers his functions to another character or changes his fundamental characterization. The film Hagrid is slightly simpler and less textured than the book Hagrid, but remains substantially complete. No score caps are triggered.
